summary: Matt Rocklin argues that Slack and other real-time chat systems are counterproductive for open source software development, despite their polish and popularity. He advocates for GitHub-based conversations because they are public, permanent, searchable, and cross-referenceable. The post outlines five key reasons why public issue trackers are superior: engaging external collaborators, recording conversations for future developers, serving users who search for answers, encouraging thoughtful discourse, and enabling cross-project references. While acknowledging Slack's value for interpersonal communication and daily team coordination, Rocklin concludes that technical discussions and long-term planning should happen in public spaces where they can benefit the broader community over time.
key_points: Real-time chat feels productive but does little for long-term community consensus building across institutions and time
Public conversations on GitHub allow external collaborators to contribute answers, questions, and context that improve solutions
Recorded conversations prevent knowledge loss when team members change and reduce repetitive discussions
Maintainers are more willing to craft high-quality answers when they can help future users through web searches
The permanence of GitHub forces more thoughtful discourse and enables cross-referencing between projects
money_quotes: Real-time chat systems make everyone feel productive (it's fun to have a face-paced technical conversation with colleagues), but do relatively little for long-term community consensus building, particularly when the users and developers of a project span a variety of institutions and a long range of time.
Most important problems are useful across many organizations, while solutions built within one organization are often overly-specific to the needs of that organization.
If you open the design process to the creators and users of the previous 14 standards, the 15th standard has a much higher probability of actually being used.
On Slack, maintainers get asked the same questions daily. We have people ask for 'a few minutes of our time' every few minutes.
Want a good answer? Pose a good question in a public place.
Because GitHub is a permanent record it forces people to think more before they write.

Slack is the easy choice. It's so much easier just to open up a one-on-one conversation with someone, chat with them for a bit, get your answer, and feel like you've had a productive experience. It feels good, but is rarely the correct choice long term.
key_insight: The short-term convenience of private real-time chat comes at the expense of long-term community knowledge building, and open source projects thrive when conversations happen in public, permanent, searchable spaces.
